items.title: Email spam filter (Bernoulli + Categorical)
items.scenario: Each email is labeled spam (1) or not spam (0)—a Bernoulli outcome per message. The model outputs P(spam) from logistic regression (Bernoulli likelihood). The sender domain might be modeled as Categorical over hundreds of domains.
items.explanation: Binary classification uses Bernoulli assumptions on the label; multi-class side features or multi-class intent use Categorical/softmax.

items.title: A/B test click counts (Binomial)
items.scenario: You show an ad to 1,000 users; 120 click. If each user clicks independently with unknown probability p, the total clicks X ~ Binomial(1000, p).
items.explanation: Binomial models fixed-n success counts; MLE of p is 120/1000 = 0.12. Confidence intervals and significance tests often assume Binomial or Normal approximation.

items.title: Support tickets per hour (Poisson)
items.scenario: A help desk receives on average 4.5 tickets per hour with independent arrivals. The count in the next hour is modeled as Poisson(λ = 4.5).
items.explanation: Poisson regression extends this when λ depends on features (time of day, product version). Over-dispersion (variance > mean) may suggest Negative Binomial instead.

items.title: Handwritten digit class (Categorical)
items.scenario: MNIST labels are digits 0–9. A softmax layer outputs π_k = P(digit = k | image) for k = 0,…,9; training minimizes categorical cross-entropy against one-hot labels.
items.explanation: Each image label is one draw from a Categorical distribution parameterized by network logits passed through softmax.

items.title: Sensor noise in regression (Gaussian)
items.scenario: A temperature sensor returns readings with small random error around the true value. Residuals after fitting a linear model look roughly symmetric and unimodal—Gaussian noise is a standard working assumption.
items.explanation: Least squares corresponds to MLE under Gaussian errors. Gaussian Naive Bayes assumes each feature is Normal within each class.
